era_context: The late 14th century in Europe was marked by the aftermath of the Black Death (1347–1353), which had killed 30–60% of Europe's population. The Hundred Years' War between England and France continued with intermittent campaigns. The Ottoman Empire was expanding in the Balkans and Anatolia. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty (founded 1368) was consolidating control over China after overthrowing the Mongol Yuan. The Papacy remained in Avignon, and the Western Church faced growing institutional criticism.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records from this period)
Americas (no European contact; indigenous records limited)

events.title: Battle of La Rochelle (English Naval Defeat)
events.region: Atlantic Ocean, off La Rochelle, France
events.description: On 22 June 1372, an English fleet under the command of John Hastings was defeated by a Castilian-French naval force off La Rochelle. This battle was a significant English naval defeat during the Hundred Years' War and marked a shift in naval power in the Atlantic. The English had previously dominated the seas, but the loss at La Rochelle demonstrated the growing naval capabilities of Castile and France. The defeat had strategic implications for English supply lines to Aquitaine and other English-held territories in France. The battle is sometimes cited as marking the end of English naval supremacy in the Atlantic.

sources.name: English and French naval records and chronicles
Historiographic consensus on Battle of La Rochelle

events.certainty_note: The Battle of La Rochelle on 22 June 1372 is well-attested in contemporary chronicles and later historical sources. The date and general outcome are not disputed.
cross_references: Related to English naval decline in the late 14th century

events.title: Ming Dynasty Establishes Tributary Relations with Southeast Asia
events.region: China and Southeast Asia (Ming Dynasty sphere)
events.description: In the early 1370s, the Ming Dynasty began establishing tributary relations with Southeast Asian states, including Siam (Thailand), Champa, and other regional powers. These diplomatic missions and tributary arrangements were part of the Ming's broader strategy to establish regional hegemony and secure trade routes. The tributary system allowed the Ming to project power and prestige without direct military conquest. By 1372, the Ming had begun sending diplomatic missions to Southeast Asia, establishing the framework for the tributary relationships that would characterize Ming foreign policy for centuries.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Southeast Asian chronicles and records (where available)
Historiographic consensus on Ming tributary system

events.certainty_note: The general timeline of Ming tributary relations with Southeast Asia is well-established, but the precise date of initial missions in 1372 is approximate. Dating is within ±2 years.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty foreign policy (1368–1644)
Related to Zheng He's later voyages (1405–1433)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of La Rochelle is sometimes overstated in significance. While it was an important English naval defeat, it did not immediately end English naval power or English involvement in the Hundred Years' War. English forces continued to operate in France and the Atlantic for decades after 1372. The battle is better understood as one of several factors contributing to the gradual shift in naval power rather than a decisive turning point.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1372 is moderate for Western European events (particularly the Battle of La Rochelle) but sparse for other regions. The battle is well-documented in contemporary chronicles and later historical sources. Ming Dynasty events are documented in Chinese sources but lack specific annual detail for 1372. Southeast Asian events are less clearly documented in Western sources and rely on later compilations or Chinese records of tributary missions.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of La Rochelle was part of the broader Hundred Years' War conflict that continued through the 1370s
Ming tributary relations established in the early 1370s provided the framework for Zheng He's later voyages
